title: Drop pydantic dependency, use stdlib dataclasses
body:
## Summary

- Replace pydantic `BaseModel` with stdlib `@dataclass` for `RiskScore`, `RiskEvent`, and `Session` models ŌĆö pydantic was only used for typed data containers with no runtime validation that wasn't already handled by the calculation logic
- Eliminate the entire dependency bootstrap problem (venv creation, pip/uv install in setup.sh, import guards in hook_handler and CLI) ŌĆö RTFI now has **zero third-party dependencies**, matching the pattern used by other Claude Code plugins
- Add `.mise.toml` to pin project Python version (3.14), so `mise install` is all that's needed for environment setup

## Motivation

The only third-party dependency (`pydantic>=2.0.0`) caused a cascading setup problem: hooks needed a venv, setup.sh needed to create the venv before the CLI could run, and macOS system Python (3.9.6) couldn't install packages without elevated permissions. Other Claude Code plugins (e.g. token-optimizer) avoid this entirely by using stdlib only.

## Changes

| Area | Change |
|------|--------|
| `scripts/rtfi_core.py` | `BaseModel` ŌåÆ `@dataclass`, `Field()` ŌåÆ `field()`, `.model_dump()` ŌåÆ `asdict()` |
| `scripts/rtfi_dashboard.py` | Same `.model_dump()` ŌåÆ `asdict()` fix (also fixes a pre-existing test failure) |
| `scripts/hook_handler.py` | Removed pydantic import guard |
| `scripts/rtfi_cli.py` | Removed pydantic import guard, setup check, and health check |
| `scripts/setup.sh` | Removed venv creation and pydantic install; validates Python version and runs CLI setup |
| `scripts/run_hook.sh` | Removed venv fallback, just calls `python3` directly |
| `.mise.toml` | New ŌĆö pins `python = "3.14"` |
| `pyproject.toml` | `dependencies = []` |
| CI/Makefile/marketplace | Removed pydantic from all install commands |
| README/docs | Updated install instructions, troubleshooting, architecture |

## Test plan

- [x] All 90 tests pass (`python3 -m pytest tests/ -v`)
- [ ] Verify `bash scripts/setup.sh` works on a clean machine with mise
- [ ] Verify `/rtfi:status` and `/rtfi:health` work without pydantic installed
- [ ] Verify dashboard launches and session detail API returns valid JSON

title: Upgrade to Django 3.x and Python 3.10+
body:
Modernizes django-form-utils to support Django 3.0-3.2 and Python 3.6+. Drops support for Django < 2.2 and Python 2.x.

## Core Changes

**Removed Python 2 compatibility layer:**
- `django.utils.six` ŌåÆ native Python 3 (`six.text_type()` ŌåÆ `str()`, `six.iteritems()` ŌåÆ `.items()`)
- `@python_2_unicode_compatible` decorator removed

**Fixed Django 3.x compatibility:**
- `BoundField` import: `django.forms.forms.BoundField` ŌåÆ `django.forms.boundfield.BoundField`
- Widget `render()` methods now accept `renderer` parameter
- Template tags use dict context instead of `template.Context`

**Rewrote `ClearableFileInput` widget rendering:**
Django 3.x moved from `format_output()` to template-based widget rendering. Overrode `render()` to maintain custom template formatting:

```python
def render(self, name, value, attrs=None, renderer=None):
    # Decompress and render subwidgets individually
    rendered_widgets = []
    for i, widget in enumerate(self.widgets):
        widget_name = '%s_%s' % (name, i)
        rendered_widgets.append(widget.render(widget_name, value[i], ...))
    
    # Apply custom template
    if current_value:
        return mark_safe(self.template % {'input': rendered_widgets[0], 'checkbox': rendered_widgets[1]})
    return mark_safe(rendered_widgets[0])
```

## Configuration Updates

- `setup.py`: Python 3.6-3.10, Django 2.2-3.2
- `tox.ini`: Updated test matrix, removed South dependency
- Test runner: `MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES` ŌåÆ `MIDDLEWARE`, added `TEMPLATES` configuration

## Test Updates

Accounts for Django 3.x behavioral changes:
- HTML output includes `required` attributes
- Field widgets are deep-copied (identity checks replaced with type checks)

All 48 tests passing on Django 3.0, 3.1, 3.2.

title: Port codebase from Python 2 to Python 3
body:
The codebase used Python 2-only syntax (`print` statements, `/` integer division), causing `SyntaxError` and runtime failures under Python 3.

## Changes

- **`print` statements ŌåÆ `print()` calls** in `Stabilization.py` and `homography.py`
- **Integer division** (`/` ŌåÆ `//`) wherever the result is used as an array index or shape dimension:
  - `Stabilization.py`: `np.zeros` shape args
  - `MeshFlow.py`: mesh grid `cols`/`rows`, median list index
  - `Optimization.py`: `range()` bounds for window size
  - `homography.py`: `VERTICAL_BORDER` computation
- **`.gitignore`**: added `src/__pycache__/`

```python
# Before (Python 2)
print method.__name__ + ' has taken: ' + str(end_time - start_time) + ' sec'
x_paths = np.zeros((old_frame.shape[0]/PIXELS, old_frame.shape[1]/PIXELS, 1))

# After (Python 3)
print(method.__name__ + ' has taken: ' + str(end_time - start_time) + ' sec')
x_paths = np.zeros((old_frame.shape[0]//PIXELS, old_frame.shape[1]//PIXELS, 1))
```

title: Week 1: remaining tasks from design doc
body:
## Summary

- Add `CREATE TABLE jobs` SQL migration with all columns matching the `Job` dataclass, plus 3 performance indexes for dequeue, lease reaping, and type/status filtering
- Add `migrate` role so the entrypoint can run migrations via `ROLE=migrate`
- Add Kubernetes Job manifest to run migrations inside minikube
- Verify end-to-end Python-to-Postgres connectivity from inside the cluster

## What changed and why

### `migrations/001_create_jobs.sql` (new)

Creates the `jobs` table with 16 columns and 3 indexes. Every column maps 1:1 from the `Job` dataclass in `models.py`. Key design decisions:

- **`UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid()`** ŌĆö UUIDs avoid sequential-ID guessing and work across distributed producers. `gen_random_uuid()` is built into Postgres 13+, no extension needed.
- **`idempotency_key TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE`** ŌĆö prevents duplicate job submission at the DB level.
- **`JSONB`** for `payload` / `result_payload` ŌĆö binary format, supports indexing, faster reads than plain JSON.
- **`TIMESTAMPTZ`** for all timestamps ŌĆö timezone-aware prevents bugs when pods run in different TZ configs.
- **`status TEXT`** not `ENUM` ŌĆö easier to evolve without a migration; valid values enforced in application code.
- **`CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` / `CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS`** throughout ŌĆö makes the migration idempotent (safe to re-run).

**The 3 indexes:**

1. **`idx_jobs_dequeue`** ŌĆö `(priority DESC, created_at ASC) WHERE status = 'queued'`
   - A **partial index** that only contains queued rows. Columns match the `ORDER BY` of the worker's `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` dequeue query exactly.
   - As most jobs move to `completed`/`dead_lettered`, this index stays small. Postgres can do an index-only scan for the dequeue query, which is the hottest path in the system.

2. **`idx_jobs_lease`** ŌĆö `(lease_expires_at) WHERE status = 'running' AND lease_expires_at IS NOT NULL`
   - A **partial index** covering only actively-running jobs with a lease.
   - The lease reaper queries `WHERE status = 'running' AND lease_expires_at < now()`. This index makes that query fast and stays small since only in-flight jobs are indexed.

3. **`idx_jobs_type_status`** ŌĆö `(job_type, status)`
   - Standard composite btree index for admin queries like "show all failed email jobs".
   - `job_type` first because it has higher cardinality and is the more common filter prefix.

### `src/taskqueue/migrate.py` (new)

Reads all `*.sql` files from `migrations/` in sorted order and executes them against the database. Uses the existing `get_connection()` from `db.py`. The migration directory is resolved via the `MIGRATIONS_DIR` env var, falling back to a relative `migrations/` path (which resolves from the Dockerfile's `WORKDIR /app`).

**Why not Alembic?** The project is in Week 1 with a single table. A simple script that runs `.sql` files in sorted order is the right abstraction level. Can be replaced with Alembic later when the schema evolves.

**Output when run:**
```
Running 001_create_jobs.sql ...
All migrations applied successfully.
```

### `entrypoint.sh` (modified)

Added `migrate)` case to dispatch to `python -m taskqueue.migrate`. This lets Kubernetes Jobs (and developers) run migrations by setting `ROLE=migrate`.

### `Dockerfile` (modified)

Added `COPY migrations/ migrations/` so SQL files are available inside the container. Without this, `migrate.py`'s path resolution would find an empty/missing directory.

### `k8s/migrate-job.yaml` (new)

Kubernetes **Job** (not Deployment) that runs the migration once and exits. Job is the correct K8s primitive for one-shot tasks.

Key settings:
- `imagePullPolicy: Never` ŌĆö uses the image built directly in minikube's Docker daemon, doesn't try to pull from Docker Hub
- `restartPolicy: Never` + `backoffLimit: 3` ŌĆö retries up to 3 times on failure (handles the race if migration starts before Postgres is ready), then stops
- `DATABASE_URL` injected via `secretKeyRef` from `postgres-secret` ŌĆö keeps credentials centralized

### `README.md` (modified)

Added "Build and deploy the task queue" section and updated the project structure listing.

## How to test on your Mac

### 1. Start minikube
```bash
minikube start
```
Starts the cluster (or resumes it if stopped). On first run, add `--driver=docker --memory=4096 --cpus=2`.

### 2. Build the Docker image inside minikube's Docker
```bash
eval $(minikube docker-env)
docker build -t taskqueue:latest .
```
`eval $(minikube docker-env)` redirects your shell's `docker` command to minikube's internal Docker daemon. This puts the image directly where Kubernetes can find it ŌĆö no registry needed. Every new terminal session needs this again.

### 3. Deploy everything
```bash
kubectl delete job taskqueue-migrate --ignore-not-found
kubectl apply -f k8s/
```
Deploys Postgres (secret, PVC, deployment, service) and the migration Job all at once. The `delete` first is needed because K8s Jobs can't be re-applied without removing the previous one; `--ignore-not-found` prevents errors on first run.

### 4. Wait for Postgres, then check migration
```bash
kubectl get pods -l app=postgres -w
# Wait until you see 1/1 Running, then Ctrl+C

kubectl wait --for=condition=complete job/taskqueue-migrate --timeout=60s
kubectl logs job/taskqueue-migrate
```
Expected output:
```
Running 001_create_jobs.sql ...
All migrations applied successfully.
```

### 5. Verify the table and indexes exist
```bash
kubectl run pg-verify --rm -it --restart=Never \
  --image=postgres:16 \
  --env="PGPASSWORD=taskqueue-dev-password" \
  -- psql -h postgres -U taskqueue -d taskqueue --pset pager=off \
  -c "SELECT column_name, data_type FROM information_schema.columns WHERE table_name='jobs' ORDER BY ordinal_position;"
```
Expected: a table listing all 16 columns with their Postgres types.

```bash
kubectl run pg-idx --rm -it --restart=Never \
  --image=postgres:16 \
  --env="PGPASSWORD=taskqueue-dev-password" \
  -- psql -h postgres -U taskqueue -d taskqueue --pset pager=off \
  -c "SELECT indexname FROM pg_indexes WHERE tablename='jobs';"
```
Expected: 5 indexes ŌĆö `jobs_pkey`, `jobs_idempotency_key_key`, `idx_jobs_dequeue`, `idx_jobs_lease`, `idx_jobs_type_status`

### 6. Verify Python-to-Postgres connection end-to-end
```bash
kubectl run py-test --rm -it --restart=Never \
  --image=taskqueue:latest \
  --image-pull-policy=Never \
  --env="DATABASE_URL=postgresql://taskqueue:taskqueue-dev-password@postgres:5432/taskqueue" \
  --command -- python -c "
from taskqueue.db import get_connection
conn = get_connection()
cur = conn.execute('SELECT count(*) FROM jobs')
print(f'Row count: {cur.fetchone()[0]}')
conn.close()
print('Connection verified.')
"
```
**Important:** The `--image-pull-policy=Never` flag is required so Kubernetes uses the locally built image instead of trying to pull from Docker Hub (which will fail).

This proves the full chain: Python code ŌåÆ psycopg ŌåÆ `DATABASE_URL` env var ŌåÆ Postgres service DNS ŌåÆ Postgres pod ŌåÆ `jobs` table.

Expected output:
```
Row count: 0
Connection verified.
```

### 7. Re-run migration to confirm idempotency
```bash
kubectl delete job taskqueue-migrate --ignore-not-found
kubectl apply -f k8s/migrate-job.yaml
kubectl wait --for=condition=complete job/taskqueue-migrate --timeout=60s
kubectl logs job/taskqueue-migrate
```
Expected: same success output ŌĆö `IF NOT EXISTS` clauses make it a no-op.

## Idempotency

The migration uses `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` and `CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS` throughout, so it is safe to run multiple times. Re-running the migration job is a no-op if the table already exists.

title: Modernize repo: src layout, type hints, ruff, GitHub Actions CI/CD
body:
## Summary

This PR brings SeisCM from its 2017 Python 2.7 era into 2025 with modern tooling and best practices.

### Changes

#### Project Structure
- **`src/` layout** ŌĆö migrated from flat `seiscm/` to `src/seiscm/`
- **`setup.py` ŌåÆ `pyproject.toml`** with `setuptools_scm` for dynamic git-tag versioning
- **`uv.lock`** for reproducible dependency resolution
- Removed `tests/__init__.py` (unnecessary with pytest)

#### Code Quality & Typing
- **Type annotations** with `TypeAlias` for colormap segment dictionaries
- **`py.typed`** marker (PEP 561) for downstream type checker support
- **`_make_cmap()`** helper extracted to DRY up colormap construction
- **Fixed** `frequency()` docstring (was copy-pasted from `phase()`)
- **Dropped** Python 2 compat (`__future__` imports, `# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-`)

#### Linting & Formatting
- **Ruff** with rules: `E/W/F/I/UP/B/SIM/RUF`
- **Pre-commit** config with ruff hooks

#### CI/CD
- **GitHub Actions** replaces dead Travis CI
- **Test matrix**: Python 3.10ŌĆō3.13
- **Dedicated lint job**
- **PyPI trusted publishing** on version tags

#### Tests
- **Expanded from 4 ŌåÆ 12 tests**: alpha validation, edge colours, cmap names, transparency
- `autouse` fixture to close matplotlib figures (no leaks)

#### Housekeeping
- **.gitignore** cleaned (removed Django/Flask/Scrapy cruft)
- **README** rewritten: CI badge, colormap table, quick-start example, dev instructions

title: Serve static session view from JSON file and move template to `templates/index.html`
body:
### Motivation
- Provide a simple, server-startup-driven viewer that reads a JSON file and renders the LLM comparison pages instead of relying on client-side paste/validation. 
- Simplify the app by moving the large HTML/JS into a Jinja template and loading sessions at startup for easier deployment.

### Description
- Replaced the inline `PAGE_TEMPLATE` and client-side validation UI with a Jinja template at `templates/index.html` and use `render_template("index.html", sessions=SESSIONS)` in `app.py`.
- Added CLI argument parsing via `argparse` so the server is started with `python app.py /path/to/data.json` and accepts `--host` and `--port` options.
- Added `load_sessions(json_path: Path)` which reads the JSON file, validates top-level shape (must be a list and each item must have a `models` list of exactly 3 entries), and returns the raw sessions; store results in a `SESSIONS` global used by the template.
- Removed previous dataclasses, the `/api/validate` endpoint, and the client-side JavaScript that performed JSON parsing/validation; the app now fails early if the JSON file is missing or malformed.
- Updated `README.md` to document the new invocation (`python app.py /path/to/data.json`) and to describe that the app renders the provided JSON on `http://127.0.0.1:5000`.

### Testing
- No automated tests were added or executed as part of this change.

title: Add TF v2->v1 compatibility, Python 3.10 quickstart, and requirements for Py310
body:
### Motivation
- Update the project to run on modern Python/TensorFlow environments by using the `tensorflow.compat.v1` bridge so existing TF1-style code continues to work under TF2 runtimes. 
- Provide a concise Quickstart for users on Python 3.10 and declare a set of known-good dependencies for that environment.

### Description
- Replace top-level `tensorflow` imports with `tensorflow.compat.v1` and enable v1 behavior in `core/model.py`, `core/networks.py`, and `pipeline/NiftiDataset.py` by calling `tf.disable_v2_behavior()`.
- Defer and guard TensorFlow import in `main.py`, adding a friendly `ModuleNotFoundError` message and switching to `tensorflow.compat.v1` plus `tf.disable_v2_behavior()` inside `main()` to improve startup diagnostics.
- Change default config path in the CLI argument to `./configs/config.json` and keep `main.py` GPU selection and session setup using a v1 `tf.Session`.
- Add a `requirements-py310.txt` file with pinned dependencies for Python 3.10 (including `tensorflow==2.15.1` and `SimpleITK==2.3.1`).
- Add a `Quickstart (Environment -> Training)` section to `README.md` with environment creation, sample `main.py` commands for training and prediction, and verification steps.

### Testing
- No automated tests were added or executed as part of this change.
- The changes are focused on compatibility and documentation updates and should be validated by running the CLI smoke commands in the new `README` on a matching environment.

Release notes

Sourced from requests's releases.

v2.33.0

2.33.0 (2026-03-25)

Announcements

  • ­¤ōŻ Requests is adding inline types. If you have a typed code base that uses Requests, please take a look at #7271. Give it a try, and report any gaps or feedback you may have in the issue. ­¤ōŻ

Security

  • CVE-2026-25645 requests.utils.extract_zipped_paths now extracts contents to a non-deterministic location to prevent malicious file replacement. This does not affect default usage of Requests, only applications calling the utility function directly.

Improvements

  • Migrated to a PEP 517 build system using setuptools. (#7012)

Bugfixes

  • Fixed an issue where an empty netrc entry could cause malformed authentication to be applied to Requests on Python 3.11+. (#7205)

Deprecations

  • Dropped support for Python 3.9 following its end of support. (#7196)

Documentation

  • Various typo fixes and doc improvements.

body:
## Summary

- **FastAPI REST API** with 10 endpoints serving captures, images, thumbnails, renders, videos, status, and config
- **Vue 3 SPA** with four views: Dashboard, Gallery (by-date + through-year modes), Videos, and Render job submission
- **Dark responsive theme** ŌĆö works on desktop and mobile
- **Third systemd service** (`timelapse-web`) running alongside capture and render
- **173 Python tests** + **4 frontend tests** passing

## Architecture

FastAPI serves both the REST API and the Vue static build from a single process (port 8080). Read-mostly ŌĆö only writes are render job submissions. Thumbnails generated on demand and cached.

```
New files:
  src/timelapse/web/           # FastAPI app + 6 route modules + thumbnails
  frontend/                    # Vue 3 + Vite SPA (14 components/views)
  systemd/timelapse-web.service
  tests/test_web_*.py          # 5 test files

Modified:
  src/timelapse/jobs.py        # time_from/time_to columns, new query methods
  src/timelapse/worker.py      # time-of-day filtering for renders
  pyproject.toml               # fastapi, uvicorn, Pillow, httpx deps
  scripts/install.sh           # includes web service
```

## Features

- **Dashboard**: system status, capture window, camera previews with live thumbnails, auto-refresh
- **Gallery**: browse by date with camera filter, "Through Year" mode (one image per day at a given time), lightbox viewer
- **Videos**: browse daily/custom renders, in-browser playback, download
- **Render**: submit jobs with date range, time-of-day filter, FPS, resolution; live job queue

## Test plan

- [x] 173 Python tests passing (API endpoints, thumbnails, path traversal, cross-service integration)
- [x] 4 frontend tests passing (API client, NavBar component)
- [x] Live tested on Pi: dashboard with real data, gallery browsing, render submission ŌåÆ worker ŌåÆ video playback
- [x] Services survive reboot

## Database migration note

Existing databases need two columns added manually:
```sql
ALTER TABLE render_jobs ADD COLUMN time_from TEXT;
ALTER TABLE render_jobs ADD COLUMN time_to TEXT;
```

title: Add GitHub Pages deployment workflow for theme gallery
body:
Automates building and deploying all email theme examples to GitHub Pages on every push to `main`, making themes browsable without cloning.

## Changes

### `.github/workflows/deploy-pages.yml`
- Two-job pipeline: `build` ŌåÆ `deploy`
- Build: Python 3.11 + `uv` (`uv pip install --system .`), runs `generate_examples.py`, uploads `examples/generated_html/` as Pages artifact
- Deploy: `actions/deploy-pages@v4` with `pages: write` + `id-token: write` permissions
- Concurrency group prevents overlapping deployments

### `generate_examples.py`
- `PYTHON_EXE` changed from hardcoded `/usr/local/bin/python` to `sys.executable` ŌĆö was broken in any non-Docker environment including CI

### `README.md`
- Deployment status badge
- Live demo link (`https://macintorsten.github.io/git-newsletter/`) with note on auto-update cadence

<!-- START COPILOT ORIGINAL PROMPT -->



<details>

<summary>Original prompt</summary>

## Objective

Create a GitHub Actions workflow that automatically builds all example HTML files and deploys them to GitHub Pages, making it easy for users to preview all available email themes without cloning the repository.

## Requirements

### 1. GitHub Actions Workflow

Create `.github/workflows/deploy-pages.yml` that:
- Triggers on pushes to `main` branch
- Triggers on manual workflow dispatch
- Sets up Python environment with required dependencies
- Runs `generate_examples.py` to build all HTML examples
- Deploys the `examples/generated_html/` directory to GitHub Pages

### 2. Workflow Steps

The pipeline should:
1. Check out the repository
2. Set up Python 3.11+
3. Install dependencies from `pyproject.toml` (using uv or pip)
4. Run `python generate_examples.py` to generate all example HTML files
5. Use the official `actions/upload-pages-artifact` and `actions/deploy-pages` actions to deploy
6. Handle permissions correctly for GitHub Pages deployment

### 3. Configuration

- Ensure the workflow has proper permissions for `pages: write` and `id-token: write`
- Configure to deploy from the `examples/generated_html/` directory
- Include the generated `index.html` as the landing page

### 4. Documentation

Update `README.md` to:
- Add a badge showing the Pages deployment status
- Add a section linking to the live GitHub Pages demo
- Mention that examples are automatically updated on each push to main

## Technical Details

- The `generate_examples.py` script already exists and generates:
  - Standard versions of all themes
  - Wide versions (700px) of all themes
  - An `index.html` gallery page
- All output goes to `examples/generated_html/`
- The workflow should use the same Python version as specified in `pyproject.toml` (>=3.11)

## Success Criteria

- GitHub Pages site is successfully deployed
- All 10 theme examples are visible in both standard and wide formats
- The index page provides easy navigation between themes
- The pipeline runs automatically on pushes to main
- The README includes a link to the live demo

The following is the prior conversation context from the user's chat exploration (may be truncated):

User: In docker compose what are the different network Modes and how does host mode work. Does the container see the hosts ip as its own, what about dns names?

Assistant: Docker Compose supports several network modes, each with different behaviors for container networking:

## Network Modes in Docker Compose

1. **`bridge` (default)**: Containers get their own network namespace and IP addresses on a private bridge network. They can communicate with each other and access the outside world through NAT.

2. **`host`**: Container shares the host's network namespace directly - no network isolation.

3. **`none`**: Container has no network access at all.

4. **`service:<service-name>`**: Container shares the network namespace of another service.

5. **`container:<container-name>`**: Shares the network namespace of another specific container.

6. **Custom networks**: User-defined bridge or overlay networks with DNS resolution between services.

## How Host Mode Works

When you use `network_mode: host` in Docker Compose:

```yaml
services:
  myapp:
    image: myimage
    network_mode: host
```

### Key Behaviors:

**IP Address:**
- Ō£ģ Yes, the container sees the host's IP address as its own
- The container shares the host's network stack completely
- Running `ifconfig` or `ip addr` inside the container shows the same interfaces and IPs as the host

**Port Mapping:**
- Port mappings (`ports:`) are **ignored** - not needed or effective
- If your app listens on port 8080, it's directly accessible on the host's IP at port 8080
- No NAT/translation occurs

**DNS Names:**
- The container uses the **host's DNS configuration** (`/etc/resolv.conf`)
- Docker's internal DNS service (for service-to-service discovery) is **NOT available**
- You cannot resolve other Docker service names when using host mode
- You get whatever DNS servers and search domains the host uses

**Hostname:**
- By default, the container sees the host's hostname
- You can override this with `hostname:` in your compose file, but it only affects the internal hostname, not network behavior

### Limitations of Host Mode:

- **No container-to-container DNS**: Service names won't resolve
- **No network isolation**: Security boundary is reduced
- **Not portable**: Works differently on Docker Desktop (Mac/Windows) vs Linux
- **Port conflicts**: Multiple containers can't use the same port since they all share the host's ports
- **Not available in swarm mode**: Only works with standalone containers

Host mode is useful for performance-critical applications or when you need to bind to specific host interfaces, but it sacrifices Docker's networking features and isolation.

User: What it Swarm mode

Assistant: ## Docker Swarm Mode

**Docker Swarm** is Docker's native **container orchestration** and **clustering** solution. It turns a group of Dock...

title: chore(deps): bump the github-actions-dependencies group with 5 updates
body:
Bumps the github-actions-dependencies group with 5 updates: | Package | From | To | | --- | --- | --- | | [actions/download-artifact](https://github.com/actions/download-artifact) | `4` | `8` | | [softprops/action-gh-release](https://github.com/softprops/action-gh-release) | `2` | `3` | | [actions/checkout](https://github.com/actions/checkout) | `4` | `6` | | [actions/setup-python](https://github.com/actions/setup-python) | `5` | `6` | | [actions/upload-artifact](https://github.com/actions/upload-artifact) | `4` | `7` | Updates `actions/download-artifact` from 4 to 8
Release notes

Sourced from actions/download-artifact's releases.

v8.0.0

v8 - What's new

[!IMPORTANT] actions/download-artifact@v8 has been migrated to an ESM module. This should be transparent to the caller but forks might need to make significant changes.

[!IMPORTANT] Hash mismatches will now error by default. Users can override this behavior with a setting change (see below).

Direct downloads

To support direct uploads in actions/upload-artifact, the action will no longer attempt to unzip all downloaded files. Instead, the action checks the Content-Type header ahead of unzipping and skips non-zipped files. Callers wishing to download a zipped file as-is can also set the new skip-decompress parameter to true.

Enforced checks (breaking)

A previous release introduced digest checks on the download. If a download hash didn't match the expected hash from the server, the action would log a warning. Callers can now configure the behavior on mismatch with the digest-mismatch parameter. To be secure by default, we are now defaulting the behavior to error which will fail the workflow run.

ESM

To support new versions of the @actions/* packages, we've upgraded the package to ESM.

What's Changed

  • Don't attempt to un-zip non-zipped downloads by @ŌĆŗdanwkennedy in actions/download-artifact#460
  • Add a setting to specify what to do on hash mismatch and default it to error by @ŌĆŗdanwkennedy in actions/download-artifact#461

Full Changelog: https://github.com/actions/download-artifact/compare/v7...v8.0.0

v7.0.0

v7 - What's new

[!IMPORTANT] actions/download-artifact@v7 now runs on Node.js 24 (runs.using: node24) and requires a minimum Actions Runner version of 2.327.1. If you are using self-hosted runners, ensure they are updated before upgrading.

Node.js 24

This release updates the runtime to Node.js 24. v6 had preliminary support for Node 24, however this action was by default still running on Node.js 20. Now this action by default will run on Node.js 24.

What's Changed

  • Update GHES guidance to include reference to Node 20 version by @ŌĆŗpatrikpolyak in actions/download-artifact#440
  • Download Artifact Node24 support by @ŌĆŗsalmanmkc in actions/download-artifact#415
  • fix: update @ŌĆŗactions/artifact to fix Node.js 24 punycode deprecation by @ŌĆŗsalmanmkc in actions/download-artifact#451
  • prepare release v7.0.0 for Node.js 24 support by @ŌĆŗsalmanmkc in actions/download-artifact#452

New Contributors

  • @ŌĆŗpatrikpolyak made their first contribution in actions/download-artifact#440
  • @ŌĆŗsalmanmkc made their first contribution in actions/download-artifact#415

Full Changelog: https://github.com/actions/download-artifact/compare/v6.0.0...v7.0.0

Release notes

Sourced from softprops/action-gh-release's releases.

v3.0.0

3.0.0 is a major release that moves the action runtime from Node 20 to Node 24. Use v3 on GitHub-hosted runners and self-hosted fleets that already support the Node 24 Actions runtime. If you still need the last Node 20-compatible line, stay on v2.6.2.

What's Changed

Other Changes ­¤öä

  • Move the action runtime and bundle target to Node 24
  • Update @types/node to the Node 24 line and allow future Dependabot updates
  • Keep the floating major tag on v3; v2 remains pinned to the latest 2.x release

If you still hit an issue after upgrading, please open a report with the bug template and include a minimal repro or sanitized workflow snippet where possible.

Bug fixes ­¤Éø

v2.6.0

It also includes the recent concurrent asset upload recovery fix, a working_directory docs sync, a checked-bundle freshness guard for maintainers, and clearer immutable-prerelease guidance where GitHub platform behavior imposes constraints on how prerelease asset uploads can be published.

Updates `actions/upload-artifact` from 4 to 7
Release notes

Sourced from actions/upload-artifact's releases.

v7.0.0

v7 What's new

Direct Uploads

Adds support for uploading single files directly (unzipped). Callers can set the new archive parameter to false to skip zipping the file during upload. Right now, we only support single files. The action will fail if the glob passed resolves to multiple files. The name parameter is also ignored with this setting. Instead, the name of the artifact will be the name of the uploaded file.

ESM

To support new versions of the @actions/* packages, we've upgraded the package to ESM.

What's Changed

title: Add project documentation (README.md)
body:
No documentation existed for this codebase. Adds a `README.md` covering the system, architecture, and usage.

## Contents

- **Physical system** ŌĆö plate geometry, material properties, Mindlin plate theory, capacitive sensor model formula
- **Project structure** ŌĆö annotated directory tree for all 13 subdirectories
- **Dependencies & installation** ŌĆö FEniCS/DOLFIN, Gmsh, Python packages; explicit callout that the code is Python 2 / FEniCS 2017.x with guidance for running on modern systems (legacy Docker image or Python 3 port)
- **Running simulations** ŌĆö step-by-step including required `condense.py` pre-step to generate reduced-order matrices
- **Module reference** ŌĆö API tables for `ActList`, `FeedBack`, `FeedForward`, `Eulero`, `Mantegazza`, `SensorFilter`, and `condense.py`
- **Simulation workflow** ŌĆö ASCII pipeline from `.geo` ŌåÆ mesh ŌåÆ FEM assembly ŌåÆ time integration ŌåÆ output
- **Output artifacts** ŌĆö `.pvd` ParaView snapshots and `.dat` displacement time-series files
- **Key parameters** ŌĆö quick-reference table for the most commonly tuned values (`rint`, `rest`, `res`, `dt`, `T`, gains, Rayleigh damping coefficients)

title: Bump requests from 2.19.0 to 2.33.0 in /App
body:
Bumps [requests](https://github.com/psf/requests) from 2.19.0 to 2.33.0.
Release notes

Sourced from requests's releases.

v2.33.0

2.33.0 (2026-03-25)

Announcements

  • ­¤ōŻ Requests is adding inline types. If you have a typed code base that uses Requests, please take a look at #7271. Give it a try, and report any gaps or feedback you may have in the issue. ­¤ōŻ

Security

  • CVE-2026-25645 requests.utils.extract_zipped_paths now extracts contents to a non-deterministic location to prevent malicious file replacement. This does not affect default usage of Requests, only applications calling the utility function directly.

Improvements

  • Migrated to a PEP 517 build system using setuptools. (#7012)

Bugfixes

  • Fixed an issue where an empty netrc entry could cause malformed authentication to be applied to Requests on Python 3.11+. (#7205)

Deprecations

  • Dropped support for Python 3.9 following its end of support. (#7196)

Documentation

title: Pure-stdlib runner with vLLM support, per-category scoring and --out dump
body:
Rebuilt SimpleBench as a zero-dependency runner after PR #1 was reverted in #2. Drops weave / litellm / fire / python-dotenv completely in favor of `urllib` + `argparse` + `concurrent.futures`.

## Summary

- **Live progress output** - every question logs when it's sent to the model and whether a `Final Answer: X` could be extracted (`[RUN ]`, `[ OK ]`, `[FAIL]`, `[ERR ]` tags), with a running `done/total` counter.
- **OpenAI-compatible endpoints** - new `--api_host` (default `localhost`), `--api_port` (default `8000`) and `--api_key` (optional) flags, so local vLLM / LM Studio / Ollama servers work out of the box. Any OpenAI-compatible `/v1/chat/completions` endpoint is fine.
- **Pure stdlib** - no more `pip install`, no more PEP 668 headaches. Runs on any Python 3.8+.
- **Per-category scoring** - dataset rows can carry a `category` field; the summary prints a breakdown plus an overall score.
- **`--out results.json`** - optional JSON dump with per-question gold/extracted/raw-response/error, for post-mortem analysis without needing a Weave dashboard.
- **German system prompt** - `system_prompt.txt` translated to German (matches the intended German eval context).

## Test plan

- [x] Smoke-tested against a local mock `/v1/chat/completions` server (happy path + `[FAIL]` on missing `Final Answer:` + `[ERR ]` on connection refused)
- [ ] Run against a real vLLM endpoint
- [ ] Run against OpenAI-hosted `gpt-4o-mini`

title: Phase 1: Foundation Infrastructure
body:
## Summary

**Phase 1: Foundation Infrastructure**  
**Goal:** Establish development environment, CI/CD pipeline, Docker security, ROS2 workspace, and Python package scaffolding for the SanGo-Mate-VRO vessel route optimization system.  
**Status:** UAT Complete Ō£ō (7/7 tests passed)

Phase 1 delivered the foundational infrastructure upon which all subsequent phases (VRO, SIM, HMI) depend.

## What Was Built

- **Docker security**: Removed `privileged: true`, externalized PostgreSQL credentials via `env_file`, no port 5432 exposure
- **CI/CD pipeline**: Two-stage GitHub Actions (PR fast gate with lint/type-check/unit-test, merge gate with docker-build/colcon-build/integration-test)
- **Python packages**: `pytest.ini` with 80% coverage threshold; `sango-vro` and `sango-sim` `pyproject.toml` with numpy/scipy/pymoo
- **ROS2 workspace**: `sanogo_msgs`, `sanogo_vro`, `sanogo_sim` packages initialized in `src/ros2_ws/`
- **Dev setup scripts**: `setup-python.sh`, `setup-ros2.sh`, `setup-node.sh` with venv isolation
- **Secrets protection**: `.env.example` template, `.gitignore` excludes `.env` files

## Changes

### Plan 01-01: Docker Security Fixes
Docker security misconfigurations remediated.

**Files:** `docker/docker-compose.yml`, `.env.example`, `.gitignore`

### Plan 01-02: Python Package Configuration  
pytest and Python package scaffolding established.

**Files:** `pytest.ini`, `src/vro/pyproject.toml`, `src/sim/pyproject.toml`

### Plan 01-03: GitHub Actions CI/CD Pipeline
Two-stage CI/CD pipeline with Trivy security scanning.

**Files:** `.github/workflows/ci.yml`

### Plan 01-04: Development Setup Scripts
Developer onboarding scripts for Python, ROS2, and Node.js environments.

**Files:** `scripts/setup-python.sh`, `scripts/setup-ros2.sh`, `scripts/setup-node.sh`, `CONTRIBUTING.md`, ROS2 packages

## Verification

- [x] UAT: 7/7 tests passed (Docker, CI/CD, Python packages, ROS2 workspace, scripts, secrets)
- [x] Maritime agents dual verification: security and barrier compliance confirmed

## Next Phase

Phase 2: VRO Core ŌĆö NSGA-III multi-objective optimizer + FFRLS adaptive control

title: Add domain-specific guardrails to all maintenance agent skill files (closes #23)
body:
## Summary
- **Router** (`maintenance.agent.md`): new Hard Limits section ŌĆö build gate, single-major-version upgrade rule, rollback required for destructive changes, workspace scope boundary, no cross-language toolchain commands
- **Per-agent constraints** woven directly into command specs (not a separate boilerplate section):
  - `java`: dual build-file detection, JDK/Spring Boot version jump blocked
  - `dotnet`: multi-TFM all-must-pass, `packages.lock.json` locked-mode check, mandatory `-v` on `dotnet add package`
  - `python`: venv active check, `==` pinning only, mypy gate if configured, `2to3` manual review required
  - `perl`: `use strict; use warnings` mandatory in every touched file, `eval BLOCK` only
  - `webservice`: no path changes on existing endpoints, no OAuth2 downgrade
  - `vulnerability`: one CVE per response for CVSS Ōēź 7.0, no suppression-as-fix
  - `test-fix`: no test deletion (use skip with reason), no assertion removal
  - `os-compat`: no hardcoded path separators, platform guards need cross-platform fallback
  - `sonarqube`: fix broken ┬¦8/┬¦9 markdown, suppression requires rule ID + justification
  - `autosar`: must read `xsi:schemaLocation` before any fix ŌĆö no assumed schema version
  - `eclipse-rcp`: closed `[x.y.z,x.y.z]` version ranges, no `file:` URIs in target platforms
- Bump version to 2.0.4

## Test plan
- [ ] Verify router Hard Limits appear after the tools table in `chatmodes/maintenance.agent.md`
- [ ] Verify each skill file has its guardrail inside the relevant command section, not as a trailing section
- [ ] Confirm sonarqube ┬¦8 and ┬¦9 no longer contain garbled markdown
- [ ] Build passes (`tsc` zero errors, VSIX packages cleanly)

Closes #23

title: Fix Flatpak build for offline SDK environment
body:
Flatpak build was failing due to missing dependencies and packages incompatible with the offline build environment and modern SDK toolchain.

## Changes

### hidapi C library
- Add `-DHIDAPI_WITH_LIBUSB=OFF` ŌĆö SDK lacks `libusb-1.0`, hidraw backend sufficient for HID

### cython-hidapi
- Patch `setup.py` to remove pinned `Cython==0.29.25` ŌĆö SDK provides 0.29.37
- Add `--without-libusb` to skip libusb backend requiring `libusb.h`

### simpleeval
- Switch from old git archive to PyPI wheel (1.0.3) ŌĆö `use_2to3` removed in setuptools 58+

### fbs/future
- Add as separate modules with pre-downloaded sources ŌĆö no network in Flatpak builds
- Install fbs with `--no-deps` ŌĆö only `fbs_runtime` needed, not PyInstaller build tools

```yaml
# Key manifest changes
- name: hidapi
  config-opts:
    - -DHIDAPI_WITH_LIBUSB=OFF

- name: python3-hidapi
  build-commands:
    - sed -i "s/setup_requires = \['Cython==0.29.25'\]/setup_requires = []/" setup.py
    - python3 setup.py install --without-libusb --prefix=${FLATPAK_DEST}

- name: python3-fbs
  build-commands:
    - pip3 install ... --no-deps  # skip PyInstaller dependency
```

title: fix: migrate to LangChain 1.2.x create_agent API, require Python 3.12+
body:
## Summary

Fixes `ImportError: cannot import name 'AgentExecutor' from 'langchain.agents'` when running with LangChain 1.2.x. The legacy `AgentExecutor` + `create_tool_calling_agent` API was removed in LangChain 1.2.x ŌĆö this PR migrates to the new `create_agent` API (which uses LangGraph under the hood).

**Key changes:**
- **Orchestrator**: Replaced `AgentExecutor`/`create_tool_calling_agent` with `create_agent` from `langchain.agents`. Agent invocation now uses the LangGraph message-based pattern (`{"messages": [...]}`) instead of `{"input": ..., "chat_history": ...}`
- **Dependencies**: `langchain>=0.3.0` ŌåÆ `>=1.2.0`, replaced `langchain-community` with `langgraph>=1.0.0`
- **Python**: Minimum bumped from 3.10 ŌåÆ 3.12 (user requirement for unsloth compatibility); `(str, Enum)` ŌåÆ `StrEnum`
- **Response extraction**: Added `_extract_response()` helper to pull the final `AIMessage` content from the LangGraph result

## Review & Testing Checklist for Human

- [ ] **Lost `AgentExecutor` guardrails** ŌĆö `max_iterations`, `handle_parsing_errors`, and `return_intermediate_steps` were all configured on the old `AgentExecutor` but have no equivalent passed to `create_agent`. `config.MAX_AGENT_ITERATIONS` and `self._verbose` are now unused dead code. Verify the agent terminates properly on complex queries and doesn't loop indefinitely with malformed tool calls from `llama3.2`.
- [ ] **`_extract_response()` edge case** ŌĆö returns `""` if the last message has no content (e.g., tool call without follow-up). Run all CLI commands (`analyze`, `query`, `chat`, `portfolio`, `news`, `market`) and confirm non-empty output.
- [ ] **End-to-end test** ŌĆö this was NOT tested against a running Ollama server. Run with `ollama serve` + `llama3.2` + `nomic-embed-text` pulled. Suggested test plan:
  1. `ai-agent portfolio` (no LLM needed)
  2. `ai-agent market -s AAPL`
  3. `ai-agent news`
  4. `ai-agent analyze` (full agent loop with tool calling)
  5. `ai-agent chat` (verify multi-turn conversation works)
- [ ] **Chat history ordering change** ŌĆö `chat()` now appends the `HumanMessage` *before* invoking the agent (previously it was appended *after*). This is arguably more correct but is a behavioral change.

### Notes
- `self._verbose` is set from config but no longer used ŌĆö `create_agent` doesn't accept a `verbose` parameter. Consider using LangGraph's `debug=True` parameter or removing the field.
- `config.MAX_AGENT_ITERATIONS` is still defined in config but no longer referenced by the orchestrator.

Link to Devin session: https://app.devin.ai/sessions/0ca3c36ea3b749a2bfa40098e3238599
Requested by: @mborets-hash

title: Port R vignette to Jupyter and add Sphinx documentation site
body:
## Summary

- Port the canonical R vignette (`vignettes/PeacoQC_Vignette.Rmd`) to an executable Jupyter notebook at `python/docs/source/vignette.ipynb`, running end-to-end against the example FCS files shipped in `inst/extdata`.
- Add a Sphinx documentation site (`python/docs/`) with installation, usage, and API reference pages, powered by `furo` + `nbsphinx` + `autosummary`.
- Add a `docs` extra under `[project.optional-dependencies]` in `python/pyproject.toml` pulling in `sphinx`, `furo`, `nbsphinx`, `myst-parser`, `ipykernel`, and `jupyter`.
- Extend `python/.gitignore` to exclude Sphinx build artifacts, autosummary-generated RSTs, and notebook runtime output directories.

## Vignette notebook coverage

The notebook mirrors each section of the R vignette against the Python API:

1. **Standard pipeline** ŌĆö `read_fcs` + `remove_margins` on raw `111.fcs` (381 margin events removed).
2. **Full `peaco_qc`** ŌĆö `determine_good_cells="all"` on `111_Comp_Trans.fcs` with CSV report writing; prints IT/MAD/consecutive percentages (10.40% removed on the default parameters).
3. **Persist + plot** ŌĆö `write_fcs` (via the `[fcs]` extra) plus an inline `plot_peaco_qc` diagnostic figure.
4. **Mass cytometry notes** ŌĆö parameter guidance (`remove_zeros=True`, `it_limit=0.6ŌĆō0.65`) as a non-executing template.
5. **Parameter tuning** ŌĆö re-run with `mad=4` (stricter) appending to the same CSV report, so the heatmap shows both runs; removal rises from 10.40% to 16.81%.
6. **`peaco_qc_heatmap`** ŌĆö rendered on both the fresh CSV and the legacy R `PeacoQC_report.txt`, with `latest_tests=True` variant.
7. **Peak-only inspection** ŌĆö `peaco_qc(..., determine_good_cells=False)` fed into `plot_peaco_qc` as the Python analogue of R's `PlotPeacoQC(display_peaks=TRUE)`.

Divergences from the R vignette are called out in the notebook prose:
- `flowCore::compensate` / `estimateLogicle` are delegated to upstream tools (pytometry / FlowKit / flowutils).
- `peaco_qc_heatmap` does not expose a `row_split` parameter.
- The R isolation tree is replaced by `sklearn.ensemble.IsolationForest`.

## Sphinx site structure

```
python/docs/
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Makefile                    # make html / make clean
ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ source/
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ conf.py                 # furo theme, autosummary, nbsphinx, napoleon
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ index.rst               # landing page + quickstart
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ installation.rst        # PyPI install, extras table, source install
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ usage.rst               # analysis modes + toctree pulling in vignette
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ api.rst                 # autosummary for all 8 public names
    ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ vignette.ipynb          # pre-executed notebook
```

Notes:
- `nbsphinx_execute = "never"` ŌĆö the notebook is pre-executed so docs builds are deterministic.
- Autosummary generates one page per public function/class under `generated/` (gitignored).
- The Napoleon extension handles the existing NumPy-style docstrings; no docstring changes were needed.

## Test plan

- [x] `pip install -e "python/[fcs]" sphinx furo nbsphinx myst-parser ipykernel jupyter` (inside a venv to avoid a Debian setuptools quirk on Ubuntu 24.04).
- [x] `jupyter nbconvert --to notebook --execute --inplace python/docs/source/vignette.ipynb` ŌĆö completes without error; all 5 matplotlib figures + stream outputs embedded.
- [x] `sphinx-build -b html python/docs/source python/docs/_build/html` ŌĆö 13 pages built; the only warnings are intersphinx proxy timeouts from the sandboxed build environment (no content issues).
- [x] Spot-checked `_build/html/index.html`, `vignette.html` (5 embedded figures in `_images/`), `api.html`, `installation.html`, and per-function pages under `generated/`. The autodoc content for `peaco_qc` includes the full parameter list and IsolationForest note.

## Reproducing locally

```bash
pip install -e "python/[fcs,docs]"
# system pandoc is required by nbsphinx (e.g. apt-get install pandoc)
cd python/docs
make html
# open _build/html/index.html
```

title: feat: AI-powered animal behavior prediction module
body:
Extends the Wildlife Monitoring System from passive detection to predictive analytics ŌĆö classifying animal behavior (hunting, migrating, grazing, resting) and forecasting movement trajectories, with a configurable alert engine that fires when predicted paths enter defined danger zones.

## Database
Four new SQLAlchemy models:
- `AnimalTrajectory` ŌĆö per-frame (x, y, confidence) coordinate history
- `BehaviorPrediction` ŌĆö behavior label + confidence + JSON-encoded forecast positions
- `AlertRule` ŌĆö species/behavior/zone/confidence-threshold filters
- `PredictedAlert` ŌĆö alert log with rule and prediction linkage

## ML Pipeline (`src/`)
- **`behavior_predictor.py`** ŌĆö 2-layer PyTorch LSTM with additive attention; autoregressively produces 10-step position forecasts from Ōēż30 historical frames; confidence decays exponentially with horizon distance. Loads pre-trained weights when available, falls back to random init.
- **`behavior_classifier.py`** ŌĆö Feature-based classifier mapping kinematic features to behavior labels; thresholds are fully configurable.
- **`trajectory_analyzer.py`** ŌĆö Computes speed, variance, acceleration, turning rate, and path straightness; also provides multi-day centroid drift analysis for migration detection.
- **`prediction_service.py`** ŌĆö Orchestrates the full pipeline: DB trajectory lookup ŌåÆ feature extraction ŌåÆ classification ŌåÆ movement prediction ŌåÆ alert rule evaluation ŌåÆ persistence.

```python
# Full pipeline in one call
result = prediction_service.run("tiger-001", "tiger")
# ŌåÆ {"behavior": "migrating", "confidence": 0.92,
#    "predicted_positions": [{x, y, confidence}, ...x10],
#    "alerts_generated": 1, "prediction_id": 42}
```

## REST API (10 new endpoints)
| Method | Path | Purpose |
|--------|------|---------|
| `POST/GET` | `/api/trajectories[/<animal_id>]` | Record / fetch trajectory points |
| `POST` | `/api/predictions/run` | Run full prediction pipeline |
| `GET` | `/api/predictions[/<id>]` | List / retrieve predictions |
| `GET/POST` | `/api/alert-rules` | Manage alert rules |
| `PATCH` | `/api/alert-rules/<id>` | Update rule |
| `GET` | `/api/predicted-alerts` | List triggered alerts |
| `PATCH` | `/api/predicted-alerts/<id>/resolve` | Resolve alert |

`create_app()` gains an optional `test_config` dict (applied before `db.create_all()`), following the standard Flask factory pattern.

## Frontend
New `/predictions` page with:
- **Run form** ŌĆö select animal ID + species, trigger prediction on demand
- **Trajectory chart** ŌĆö Chart.js scatter plot overlaying observed path and predicted path
- **Behavior breakdown** ŌĆö polar area chart of per-label confidence scores
- **Alert panel** ŌĆö live unresolved predicted alerts with inline resolve button
- **Rules CRUD** ŌĆö inline form to create alert rules; rules table

Navbar updated on all pages.

## Tests
54 new tests covering all layers:
- `test_trajectory_analyzer.py` ŌĆö feature extraction, migration detection
- `test_behavior_classifier.py` ŌĆö label assignment, custom thresholds, edge cases
- `test_behavior_predictor.py` ŌĆö output shape, determinism, sequence padding/trimming, weight fallback
- `test_api_predictions.py` ŌĆö full endpoint integration tests with isolated per-test SQLite DBs

<!-- START COPILOT ORIGINAL PROMPT -->



<details>

<summary>Original prompt</summary>

## Overview
Enhance the Wildlife Monitoring System with advanced AI-powered animal behavior prediction capabilities. This feature will transition from simple detection to intelligent predictive analytics.

## Features to Implement

### 1. Behavior Prediction Module
- **Movement Direction Prediction**: LSTM/Transformer-based temporal model to predict where animals are heading
- **Hunting Behavior Detection**: Classify animal behavior (hunting, grazing, migrating, resting)
- **Migration Pattern Analysis**: Identify seasonal patterns and long-term movement trends
- **Alert System**: Trigger notifications when animals move toward populated areas

### 2. Core Components

#### A. Database Schema Updates (PostgreSQL)
- `animal_trajectories` table: Store coordinate history with timestamps
- `behavior_predictions` table: Store predicted behaviors with confidence scores
- `alert_rules` table: Configurable rules for different species and behaviors
- `predicted_alerts` table: Log generated alerts

#### B. Python Backend (Flask)
- `models/behavior_predictor.py`: LSTM model for movement prediction
- `models/behavior_classifier.py`: CNN/ML classifier for hunting/behavior detection
- `services/prediction_service.py`: Orchestrates predictions and alerts
- `routes/predictions.py`: REST API endpoints
- `utils/trajectory_analyzer.py`: Analyzes movement patterns

#### C. Frontend (React/JavaScript)
- Trajectory visualization with predicted paths
- Behavior classification display with confidence metrics
- Alert notification system with danger zones
- Historical pattern charts

## Technical Details

### Movement Prediction Model
- Input: Last 30 frames of animal coordinates (x, y, confidence)
- Output: Next 10 frames predicted coordinates + confidence
- Architecture: LSTM with 2 hidden layers, attention mechanism
- Training data: Historical tracking data from detection system

### Behavior Classification
- Input: Movement features (speed, acceleration, direction changes, spatial context)
- Output: Behavior class (hunting, migrating, grazing, resting, other)
- Uses ensemble of features: trajectory curvature, speed variance, proximity to resources

### Alert Logic
- Configurable danger zones (villages, roads, human settlements)
- Species-specific threat levels
- Alert escalation based on prediction confidence

## Implementation Steps

1. Create database migrations for new tables
2. Implement behavior prediction models (Python/TensorFlow)
3. Build prediction service with real-time processing
4. Add REST API endpoints for predictions and alerts
5. Create React components for visualization
6. Add comprehensive tests and documentation
7. Deploy with Docker configuration

## Success Criteria
- Behavior predictions return results in <2 seconds (maintain existing performance)
- Achieve 85%+ accuracy on held-out test dataset
- Alert system detects dangerous proximity with 90%+ precision
- System handles 1000+ animals simultaneously
- Full integration with existing detection pipeline

Instead of just detecting animalsŌĆ”

Movement direction
Hunting behavior
Migration patterns

If a tiger is moving toward a village ŌåÆ send alert

Let me help you create a structured plan for implementing this. First, let me explore your repository to understand the current architecture and suggest how to integrate this new feature.

- **Hunting behavior detection** - Is it in hunting mode?
- **Migration pattern analysis** - Long-term movement trends
- **Alert system** - Notify when threats approach populated areas (e.g., tiger moving toward village)

## ­¤Ä» Recommended Implementation Approach

Given your tech stack (Python 52.5%, Flask, PostgreSQL), here's how I'd structure this:

### 1. **Model Layer** (Python)
- Use a **temporal sequence model** (LSTM/Transformer) to track animal coordinates over time
- Train on historical movement data to predict next positions
- Classify behaviors using pose estimation + movement patterns

### 2. **Backend Enhancement** (Python/Flask)

- Store movement histories in PostgreSQL
- Create alert rules engine (configurable zones, species, behaviors)

title: feat: complete Smart Portfolio system ŌĆō Bitget-style dashboard, API & Telegram
body:
## Overview

Full implementation of the Smart Portfolio system modelled after Bitget's interface, connected to MEXC via API.

---

## Web Dashboard (Next.js)

Multi-tab single-page app replacing the previous static HTML dashboard.

**Dashboard tab**
- Pie chart (current asset distribution) + Line chart (portfolio value over time)
- KPI cards: total USDT, P&L (amount + %), last rebalance timestamp, asset count
- Asset table: symbol, target%, actual% with progress bar, deviation badge, USDT value, balance/price
- History log: last 50 operations with BUY/SELL badges, paper-trading indicator
- Controls: Start / Pause bot, ­¤öä Manual Rebalance, Ō¼ć’ĖÅ CSV export

**Create Bot tab**
- Mode selector: Recommended (4 presets) or Manual
- Recommended presets: Top 3 Coins, DeFi Portfolio, Layer 1 Mix, Balanced 5
- Asset editor: 2ŌĆō10 assets, add/remove, equal-distribution button, duplicate-symbol guard
- Rebalance mode picker with per-mode config:
  - Proportional: threshold 1%/3%/5%
  - Timed: daily/weekly/monthly + UTC hour
  - Unbalanced: manual only
- Investment amount with minimum hint
- Toggles: Sell at Termination, Enable Asset Transfer, Paper Trading (­¤¦¬)
- Saves config and auto-starts bot on submit

**Settings tab**
- Full edit of assets, allocations, rebalance mode, amount at any time
- Bot name is read-only (Bitget behaviour ŌĆö create a new bot to rename)

**New shared files**
- `web/src/components/Navbar.tsx` ŌĆō tab navigation + bot status indicator
- `web/src/components/Dashboard.tsx` ŌĆō full dashboard
- `web/src/components/CreateBot.tsx` ŌĆō bot creation wizard
- `web/src/components/Settings.tsx` ŌĆō settings editor
- `web/src/components/AssetRow.tsx` ŌĆō reusable asset table row
- `web/src/lib/api.ts` ŌĆō typed API client (all endpoints)

---

## FastAPI Backend (`api/main.py`)

- `GET /api/recommended` ŌĆō returns 4 built-in portfolio templates
- `ConfigUpdate` model: added `timed_hour` field (0ŌĆō23 UTC)
- Timed rebalancer loop now passes `target_hour` to `next_run_time()`

---

## Rebalance Engine (`smart_portfolio.py`)

- `next_run_time()` accepts `target_hour` parameter
- Daily/weekly/monthly schedules now fire at the configured UTC hour instead of immediately

---

## Telegram Bot (`telegram_bot.py`)

- **Richer rebalance notifications**: timestamp, portfolio total, P&L, per-asset deviation alongside BUY/SELL amounts
- **Timed loop**: passes `target_hour` to scheduler
- **`/history [N]`**: accepts optional count argument (1ŌĆō50, default 10); shows full trade details per operation; auto-truncates at 4000 chars
- **`/help`**: updated with full command reference and usage notes

---

## Deployment Files

- **`Procfile`**: `worker` (Telegram bot) + `web` (FastAPI) ŌĆö both run on Railway
- **`config.json`**: added `rebalance.timed.hour = 10`
- **`README.md`**: full setup guide, command reference, config schema, Railway + Vercel deployment instructions

---

## Testing

```bash
# TypeScript build
cd web && npm run build   # Ō£ģ clean

# Python syntax
python -m py_compile api/main.py smart_portfolio.py telegram_bot.py  # Ō£ģ clean

# Run locally
uvicorn api.main:app --port 8000
cd web && npm run dev
```

title: Bump requests from 2.32.4 to 2.33.0
body:
Bumps [requests](https://github.com/psf/requests) from 2.32.4 to 2.33.0.
Release notes

Sourced from requests's releases.

v2.33.0

2.33.0 (2026-03-25)

Announcements

  • ­¤ōŻ Requests is adding inline types. If you have a typed code base that uses Requests, please take a look at #7271. Give it a try, and report any gaps or feedback you may have in the issue. ­¤ōŻ

Security

  • CVE-2026-25645 requests.utils.extract_zipped_paths now extracts contents to a non-deterministic location to prevent malicious file replacement. This does not affect default usage of Requests, only applications calling the utility function directly.

Improvements

  • Migrated to a PEP 517 build system using setuptools. (#7012)

Bugfixes

  • Fixed an issue where an empty netrc entry could cause malformed authentication to be applied to Requests on Python 3.11+. (#7205)

Deprecations

  • Dropped support for Python 3.9 following its end of support. (#7196)

Documentation

  • Various typo fixes and doc improvements.

New Contributors

  • @ŌĆŗM0d3v1 made their first contribution in psf/requests#6865
  • @ŌĆŗaminvakil made their first contribution in psf/requests#7220
  • @ŌĆŗE8Price made their first contribution in psf/requests#6960

  • @ŌĆŗmagsen made their first contribution in psf/requests#6553
  • @ŌĆŗRohan5commit made their first contribution in psf/requests#7227

Full Changelog: https://github.com/psf/requests/blob/main/HISTORY.md#2330-2026-03-25

v2.32.5

2.32.5 (2025-08-18)

Bugfixes

  • The SSLContext caching feature originally introduced in 2.32.0 has created a new class of issues in Requests that have had negative impact across a number of use cases. The Requests team has decided to revert this feature as long term maintenance of it is proving to be unsustainable in its current iteration.

Deprecations

  • Added support for Python 3.14.
  • Dropped support for Python 3.8 following its end of support.

title: Bump requests from 2.31.0 to 2.33.0
body:
Bumps [requests](https://github.com/psf/requests) from 2.31.0 to 2.33.0.
Release notes

Sourced from requests's releases.

v2.33.0

2.33.0 (2026-03-25)

Announcements

  • ­¤ōŻ Requests is adding inline types. If you have a typed code base that uses Requests, please take a look at #7271. Give it a try, and report any gaps or feedback you may have in the issue. ­¤ōŻ

Security

  • CVE-2026-25645 requests.utils.extract_zipped_paths now extracts contents to a non-deterministic location to prevent malicious file replacement. This does not affect default usage of Requests, only applications calling the utility function directly.

Improvements

  • Migrated to a PEP 517 build system using setuptools. (#7012)

Bugfixes

  • Fixed an issue where an empty netrc entry could cause malformed authentication to be applied to Requests on Python 3.11+. (#7205)

Deprecations

  • Dropped support for Python 3.9 following its end of support. (#7196)

Documentation

  • Various typo fixes and doc improvements.

title: fix: repair TCP port-remapping slow path
body:
## Summary

Fixes #2. Four bugs in the TCP port-remapping slow path. The slow path (triggered when the virtual port is already taken) had not been tested (all existing tests ran sequentially, so the port was always free by the time the second sandbox started).

## Bugs fixed

**Bug 1 - `_allocate_real_port` held socket blocked the child's bind**

The parent kept the allocated real port socket open to prevent stealing, then
rewrote the child's sockaddr to that port and sent CONTINUE. The child's `bind()` hit `EADDRINUSE` because the parent was already holding the same
address:port (only released later in `PortMap.close()`). 
Fixed by closing it immediately, accepting the same tiny race window already mentioned in `_try_reserve_port`.

**Bug 2 - `os.pidfd_open` not available in Python build**

`fixup_getsockname` used `os.pidfd_open` which raises `AttributeError` on some builds. 
Fixed by using `_libc.syscall(_NR_PIDFD_OPEN)` directly, consistent with the
rest of the codebase. In the future, it makes sense to separate all syscalls into
a different file like `_syscall.py`, since an identical `_NR_PIDFD_OPEN` was
found in `_context.py`.

**Bug 3 - `fixup_getsockname` leaked the duplicated fd**

`s.detach()` does not call `os.close()`, so `local_fd` was never closed on the
success path. The leaked fd kept the child's socket alive after the child
exited, holding the port bound in the parent. Fixed by moving\ `os.close(local_fd)` into the `finally` block.

**Bug 4 - `NotifSupervisor.stop()` never called `port_map.close()`**

Proxy listener sockets were left running after sandbox teardown. Fixed by
calling `port_map.close()` at the end of `stop()`.

## Tests

Two new tests added to `TestPortRemap`:

- `test_slow_path_host_holds_virtual_port`: parent process holds the virtual
  port, forcing the slow path without threads or timing dependencies.
- `test_slow_path_two_concurrent_sandboxes`: two sandboxes run concurrently;  sandbox 1 holds the port open while sandbox 2 must remap.

title: Python 3 Migration and Architectural Refactor (v0.2.0)
body:
This PR completes the migration of PyStormTracker to modern Python 3 standards.

### Key Changes:
- **Python 3 & Type Hinting**: Fully migrated from Python 2; added comprehensive type hints verified by mypy and uff.
- **Library Migration**: Switched from deprecated PyNIO to 
etCDF4. Fixed issues with automatic coordinate scaling.
- **Architectural Refactor**: Organized code into models/ (data structures) and simple/ (core logic).
- **Modern Parallelization**: Added **Dask** as the default backend with automatic worker detection, alongside existing **MPI** support.
- **CLI Enhancements**: Modernized the CLI using rgparse and added human-readable CSV export (datetime strings, 4-decimal precision).
- **Testing**: Integrated 17 unit and integration tests verifying consistency across Serial, Dask, and MPI backends.
- **Documentation**: Updated README with professional history (NCAR SIParCS 2015), technical methodology, and formal academic citations (Yau and Chang, 2020).

title: refactor: migrate from dataclasses to Pydantic models
body:
## Summary

This PR refactors the configuration models from Python dataclasses to Pydantic models, providing better validation, clearer error messages, and simplified configuration loading.

## Changes

### Models (`models.py`)
- Replaced all `@dataclass` decorators with Pydantic `BaseModel`
- Converted `__post_init__` validation to `@field_validator` and `@model_validator`
- Updated `field()` to `Field()` for default factories
- Added `model_config` to `ReplicationConfig` to handle schema field shadowing

### Config Manager (`manager.py`)
- Simplified `load_config()` to use deep merge + Pydantic validation
- Removed manual parsing of nested config structures (Pydantic handles this)
- Added `_save_defaults()` function to generate defaults.yaml from Pydantic models
- Added `main()` function to allow regenerating defaults.yaml via CLI
- Added `DefaultsReplicationConfig` to imports

### Tests
- Updated tests to expect `ValidationError` instead of `ValueError` for Pydantic validation
- Added `from pydantic import ValidationError` imports
- Updated error message patterns to match Pydantic's error format

### Dependencies
- Added `pydantic>=2.0.0` to pyproject.toml

## Benefits

1. **Better Validation**: Pydantic provides automatic type checking and validation with clear error messages
2. **Simplified Code**: Reduced manual parsing logic in `load_config()` from ~160 lines to ~80 lines
3. **Type Safety**: Better IDE support and type checking
4. **Defaults Management**: Can now regenerate defaults.yaml from code using `python -m mongo_replication.config.manager`
5. **Deep Merge**: Simplified deep merging of defaults with user configs

## Testing

All 270 unit tests pass:
- Ō£ģ All config model tests
- Ō£ģ All config transformation tests
- Ō£ģ All other unit tests

Run tests with:
```bash
uv run pytest tests/unit/ -v
```

title: Fix for Empty except
body:
To fix the problem, we should (1) use correct Python 3 exception syntax and (2) avoid an empty `except` that only `pass`-es without explanation. The minimal behavior-preserving fix is to catch `(TypeError, ValueError)` and either log the error or, if logging is considered too noisy, add a clear comment explaining why errors are intentionally ignored. Since we cannot assume an existing logging setup, but PyQt applications commonly print or use `QMessageBox` selectively, the least invasive and still CodeQL-acceptable approach is to keep ignoring the error but add an explanatory comment and use the tuple exception syntax. That way, malformed breadth data from `QSettings` is skipped explicitly and the rest of the app continues unchanged.

Concretely, in `app/main_window.py` around lines 621ŌĆō624, replace:

```py
                try:
                    self._col_widths[tab] = [int(w) for w in raw]
                except TypeError, ValueError:
                    pass
```

with:

```py
                try:
                    self._col_widths[tab] = [int(w) for w in raw]
                except (TypeError, ValueError):
                    # Ignore malformed or non-integer column width settings and fall back to defaults.
                    pass
```

This keeps all existing functionality (bad settings are ignored) while satisfying the static analysis rule and fixing the Python 3 exception syntax.


_Suggested fixes powered by Copilot Autofix. Review carefully before merging._

title: Fix CI #16: resolve pylint violations and Python 3.13 compatibility
body:
CI #16 failed due to pylint violations across 6 files (exit code 1, 9.46/10 score) and Python 3.13 version constraint mismatch. All issues are resolved without disabling pylint globally.

## What's Changed

### Circular import eliminated (`diffsync/models.py` Ōåö `diffsync/adapters.py`)
- `_is_zabbix_target()` previously imported `ZabbixRemoteAdapter` at call time to do `isinstance` ŌĆö causing `R0401` cyclic-import and `C0415` import-outside-toplevel
- Replaced with a marker attribute check: `getattr(adapter, "IS_ZABBIX_REMOTE", False)`
- Added `IS_ZABBIX_REMOTE = True` to `ZabbixRemoteAdapter`; compatible with `MagicMock(spec=ZabbixRemoteAdapter)` in tests

### Deferred imports moved to module top-level
| File | Symbol | Pylint code |
|------|--------|-------------|
| `diffsync/models.py` | `get_zabbix_client_from_config` | C0415 (├Ś3) |
| `diffsync/adapters.py` | `nautobot.dcim.models.Device` | C0415 |
| `jobs.py` | `django.conf.settings`, `DataMapping` | C0415 (├Ś4) |
| `utils/zabbix.py` | `django.conf.settings` | C0415 |

Tests that patched `utils.zabbix.get_zabbix_client_from_config` for model CRUD tests updated to patch `diffsync.models.get_zabbix_client_from_config` ŌĆö the correct location after top-level import.

### Inline pylint disables for intentional patterns
- `broad-exception-caught` (W0718) on two adapter catch blocks ŌĆö alongside existing `# noqa: BLE001`
- `too-few-public-methods` (R0903) on `Meta` inner classes in `jobs.py` and test class in `test_zabbix_diff.py`
- `too-many-arguments` / `too-many-locals` (R0913/R0914) on `ZabbixClient.build_interface` and `upsert_host`

### Test cleanup
- All local imports inside test methods moved to module top-level
- Empty-sequence assertion: `list(...) == []` ŌåÆ `not any(...)` (C1803)

### Python 3.13 support (`pyproject.toml` + `poetry.lock`)
- Updated `requires-python` from `>=3.10,<3.13` to `>=3.10,<3.14` to match CI matrix which tests Python 3.13
- Updated `[tool.poetry.dependencies] python` from `>=3.10,<3.13` to `>=3.10,<3.14`
- Added `Programming Language :: Python :: 3.13` classifier
- Regenerated `poetry.lock` via `poetry lock` (v2.1.3) to reflect the updated Python version constraint

**Note**: The `unittest (3.10, postgresql, stable)` failure in CI run #18 was a transient Docker timeout pulling `ghcr.io/nautobot/nautobot-dev:stable-py3.10`, not a code issue.

Release notes

Sourced from python-dotenv's releases.

v1.2.2

Added

  • Support for Python 3.14, including the free-threaded (3.14t) build. (#)

Changed

  • The dotenv run command now forwards flags directly to the specified command by @ŌĆŗbbc2 in theskumar/python-dotenv#607
  • Improved documentation clarity regarding override behavior and the reference page.
  • Updated PyPy support to version 3.11.
  • Documentation for FIFO file support.
  • Support for Python 3.9.

Fixed

  • Improved set_key and unset_key behavior when interacting with symlinks by @ŌĆŗbbc2 in #790c5
  • Corrected the license specifier and added missing Python 3.14 classifiers in package metadata by @ŌĆŗJYOuyang in theskumar/python-dotenv#590

Breaking Changes

  • dotenv.set_key and dotenv.unset_key used to follow symlinks in some situations. This is no longer the case. For that behavior to be restored in all cases, follow_symlinks=True should be used.

  • In the CLI, set and unset used to follow symlinks in some situations. This is no longer the case.

  • dotenv.set_key, dotenv.unset_key and the CLI commands set and unset used to reset the file mode of the modified .env file to 0o600 in some situations. This is no longer the case: The original mode of the file is now preserved. Is the file needed to be created or wasn't a regular file, mode 0o600 is used.

title: Add deployment config, switch DB to env-driven in-memory SQLite with StaticPool, and update dependencies
body:
### Motivation
- Provide explicit Python runtime and Procfile for predictable deployment behavior and Heroku-style starts. 
- Make the database engine configurable via `DATABASE_URL` and use an in-memory SQLite default with `StaticPool` so the engine works reliably across threads (e.g. Uvicorn) and tests. 
- Align and pin dependency versions and add `greenlet` for compatibility with SQLAlchemy.

### Description
- Added `.python-version` and `runtime.txt` set to `3.12.10` and added a `Procfile` containing `web: uvicorn app.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port $PORT`.
- Modified `app/database.py` to read `DATABASE_URL` from the environment with a default of `sqlite://`, imported `os` and `StaticPool`, and configured `create_engine` with `poolclass=StaticPool` and `connect_args={"check_same_thread": False}`.
- Updated `requirements.txt` to pin `fastapi==0.100.0`, `uvicorn==0.22.0`, `pydantic==1.10.13`, kept `sqlalchemy==2.0.36`, and added `greenlet==3.0.0`.

### Testing
- Ran the project's automated test suite with `pytest` and all tests passed.

title: chore: consolidate release workflow into single job
body:
## Objective
The release workflow runs Python setup and checkout twice ŌĆö once for the test job and once for the publish job. This wastes CI time on duplicate environment setup for every release.

## Approach
Merge the two jobs (test ŌåÆ publish) into a single job that runs test ŌåÆ build ŌåÆ publish sequentially. Remove the `environment: release` setting which was not configured on PyPI trusted publisher.

## Evidence
Compared before/after workflow structure:

| Scenario | Before | After |
|----------|--------|-------|
| Python setup calls | 2 (test job + publish job) | 1 |
| Checkout calls | 2 | 1 |
| Test gate before publish | Yes (separate job `needs`) | Yes (sequential steps) |
| Environment setting | `release` (not configured) | Removed |

<!-- This is an auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->

## Summary by CodeRabbit

* **Chores**
  * Streamlined the release workflow by consolidating test and publish steps into a unified process for improved efficiency.

The build fails when installing aiogram 3.3.0 on Python 3.13 because pydantic-core (a transitive dependency) errors during Rust compilation with a ForwardRef._evaluate() incompatibility introduced in Python 3.13.

## Solution

Downgraded aiogram from 3.3.0 to 3.2.0 in requirements.txt. Version 3.2.0 resolves against a compatible version of pydantic-core that builds cleanly on Python 3.13, unblocking deployment.

title: Project scaffolding ŌĆō Flask app factory, config, and database setup
body:
Bootstraps the entire Python project from an empty repo: Poetry project, Flask app factory, SQLite database layer, schema, smoke tests, and CI.

### Project config
- `pyproject.toml` ŌĆö Poetry with `Flask ^3.1`, `APScheduler ^3.10`, `requests ^2.31`, `openai ^2.0`, `python-dotenv ^1.0`; dev deps `pytest ^8.0`, `mypy ^1.0`
- `config.py` ŌĆö all settings read from env vars (`OPENAI_API_BASE`, `OPENAI_API_KEY`, `DATABASE_URL` ŌåÆ `data/metadata.db`, `SECRET_KEY`, cron intervals)
- `.env.example` ŌĆö placeholder values for all required variables with inline time-unit comments; cron defaults: metadata daily (1440 min), ontologies monthly (720 h), tools weekly (168 h)

### Application factory (`app/__init__.py`)
- `create_app(config=None)` loads `config.py`, wires DB teardown, registers CLI, starts `BackgroundScheduler` (suppressed when `TESTING=True`)

### Database layer
- `app/db/sqlite.py` ŌĆö uses `pathlib.Path` throughout (no `os`); `get_db()` (Flask `g`-scoped connection, works for both HTTP requests and APScheduler background jobs via `with app.app_context()`); `init_db()` (idempotent, executes `schema.sql`, not a migration tool); `close_db()` (teardown hook); no sqlite-vector extension (deferred to ontology issue)
- `app/db/schema.sql` ŌĆö `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` for `users`, `sessions`, `metadata_cache`, `semantic_tools`
  - `metadata_cache` stores only URL-level crawl state (`content_hash`, `structural_summary`, `last_crawled_at`) ŌĆö no `result_json`, since results are per-user (influenced by individual prompts) and live exclusively in `sessions.result_json`
- `flask db init` CLI command calls `init_db()`

### Tests (`tests/test_app.py`)
Five smoke tests covering app instantiation, config override, table creation, and idempotent `init_db()` calls ŌĆö all using an in-memory SQLite database.

```python
app = create_app({"TESTING": True, "DATABASE_URL": ":memory:"})
# init_db() creates users / sessions / metadata_cache / semantic_tools
# calling init_db() twice does not raise (IF NOT EXISTS guards)
```

### CI (`.github/workflows/ci.yml`)
GitHub Actions workflow running `pytest tests/ -v` and `mypy app tests` on every push and pull request.

Full Changelog: https://github.com/psf/requests/blob/main/HISTORY.md#2330-2026-03-25

v2.32.5

2.32.5 (2025-08-18)

Bugfixes

  • The SSLContext caching feature originally introduced in 2.32.0 has created a new class of issues in Requests that have had negative impact across a number of use cases. The Requests team has decided to revert this feature as long term maintenance of it is proving to be unsustainable in its current iteration.

Deprecations

  • Added support for Python 3.14.
  • Dropped support for Python 3.8 following its end of support.
Changelog

Sourced from requests's changelog.

2.33.0 (2026-03-25)

Announcements

  • ­¤ōŻ Requests is adding inline types. If you have a typed code base that uses Requests, please take a look at #7271. Give it a try, and report any gaps or feedback you may have in the issue. ­¤ōŻ

Security

  • CVE-2026-25645 requests.utils.extract_zipped_paths now extracts contents to a non-deterministic location to prevent malicious file replacement. This does not affect default usage of Requests, only applications calling the utility function directly.

Improvements

  • Migrated to a PEP 517 build system using setuptools. (#7012)

Bugfixes

  • Fixed an issue where an empty netrc entry could cause malformed authentication to be applied to Requests on Python 3.11+. (#7205)

Deprecations

  • Dropped support for Python 3.9 following its end of support. (#7196)

Documentation

  • Various typo fixes and doc improvements.

2.32.5 (2025-08-18)

Bugfixes

  • The SSLContext caching feature originally introduced in 2.32.0 has created a new class of issues in Requests that have had negative impact across a number of use cases. The Requests team has decided to revert this feature as long term maintenance of it is proving to be unsustainable in its current iteration.

Deprecations

  • Added support for Python 3.14.
  • Dropped support for Python 3.8 following its end of support.
